---
title: "12 · Graphs and Their Algorithms"
subtitle: "Networks of nodes and edges — and how to traverse them"
lesson-id: "t5.graphs-algorithms"
tier: 5
prerequisites: ["t5.sets-logic-proofs", "t3.combinatorics-counting"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["databases", "ai-ml", "systems", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 25
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [11 · Sets, Logic, Proofs](../11-sets-logic-proofs/index.qmd)?
2. Have you used a map app, social network, or family tree? Those are
   graphs.
3. Comfortable with set notation?
:::

## Touch

A **graph** is a set $V$ of **vertices** with a set $E$ of **edges**
connecting pairs.

```{ojs}
viewof gSel = Inputs.select(["complete graph K₄", "cycle C₅", "tree (5 nodes)", "social-network-style"], {value: "complete graph K₄", label: "Graph type:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let info;
  switch(gSel) {
    case "complete graph K₄": info = "K_n: every pair of vertices connected. K₄ has 4 vertices, 6 edges. Edge count for K_n: n(n−1)/2 (combinations)."; break;
    case "cycle C₅": info = "C_n: each vertex connected to two neighbours, forming a single closed loop. C₅ has 5 vertices, 5 edges."; break;
    case "tree (5 nodes)": info = "Tree: connected graph with no cycles. n vertices ⇒ exactly n−1 edges. 5 nodes ⇒ 4 edges."; break;
    case "social-network-style": info = "Random sparse graph. Real-world social networks: degree distribution roughly power-law (few hubs, many leaves)."; break;
  }
  return html`<div style="margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:1rem; color:#c2185b; text-align:center;">${info}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

Graphs model: roads, social networks, web pages, neural networks,
chemical molecules, dependencies between tasks.

## Wonder

1. How many edges does the complete graph $K_n$ have?
2. A tree on $n$ vertices has exactly $n - 1$ edges. Why?
3. Given a graph, how do you find the shortest path between two
   vertices?

## Discover

### Problem 1

A **graph** $G = (V, E)$ has vertex set $V$ and edge set $E \subseteq
\binom{V}{2}$ (unordered pairs). The **degree** of a vertex is the
number of edges touching it.

For the complete graph $K_4$: $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$, $E$ = all pairs.
How many edges? Degree of each vertex?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|E| = \binom{4}{2} = 6$. Each vertex is connected to 3 others, so
degree 3.

$K_n$ has $\binom{n}{2} = n(n-1)/2$ edges; every vertex has degree
$n - 1$.
</details>

### Problem 2

**Handshake lemma.** Sum of all vertex degrees = $2 |E|$ (each edge
contributes to *two* vertices). Verify for $K_4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sum: $4 \cdot 3 = 12 = 2 \cdot 6$ ✓.

**Corollary:** the number of odd-degree vertices is even.

(This is a tiny but very useful structural fact. Proof of the
handshake lemma: count edge-endpoint incidences in two ways.)
</details>

### Problem 3

A **path** is a sequence of distinct vertices $v_0 v_1 \cdots v_k$
with each consecutive pair connected. A **cycle** is a path that
returns to start. A graph is **connected** if any two vertices have a
path between them.

A **tree** is a connected graph with **no cycles**. Show that a tree
on $n$ vertices has exactly $n - 1$ edges.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Induction on $n$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Base:** $n = 1$: 0 edges = $1 - 1$ ✓.

**Step:** A tree $T$ on $n$ vertices has a *leaf* (vertex of degree
1; trees always have leaves). Remove the leaf and its edge: the
remaining graph $T'$ has $n - 1$ vertices, is still connected and
acyclic, hence a tree. By IH, $T'$ has $n - 2$ edges. So $T$ has
$n - 1$ edges. $\square$

Trees are the *minimal* connected graphs.
</details>

### Problem 4

**Breadth-First Search (BFS)**: explore neighbours level by level. Use
a *queue*. Finds the **shortest path** in unweighted graphs.

Sketch the BFS exploration order from vertex 1 in this graph:
$1 - 2, 1 - 3, 2 - 4, 3 - 4, 4 - 5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Level 0: {1}. Level 1: {2, 3}. Level 2: {4}. Level 3: {5}.

BFS order: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Distance from 1 to 5: 3.
</details>

### Problem 5

**Dijkstra's algorithm** finds shortest paths in *weighted* graphs.
At each step, pick the unvisited vertex with smallest tentative
distance; update its neighbours' distances.

Why does Dijkstra fail with negative edge weights?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Once you "finalise" a vertex's distance, you don't reconsider it.
A future negative edge might allow a shorter path.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Dijkstra assumes that once a vertex's shortest distance is
*finalised*, it cannot be improved. With negative edges, a route
through future vertices might shorten an already-finalised vertex.

**Bellman-Ford algorithm** handles negative edges (allows
$O(VE)$ complexity vs Dijkstra's $O((V+E) \log V)$). Negative
*cycles* break shortest paths entirely (you can loop infinitely).
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **graph** $G = (V, E)$. Edges may be **directed** or **undirected**,
**weighted** or unweighted.

**Degree** of a vertex: number of edges incident to it.
**Handshake lemma:** $\sum_{v} \deg(v) = 2 |E|$.

**Path:** sequence of distinct vertices, each consecutive pair an
edge.
**Cycle:** path returning to start.
**Connected graph:** every pair has a path.

**Tree:** connected, no cycles. $n$ vertices ⇒ $n - 1$ edges.

**Common graph algorithms:**

- **BFS** (breadth-first search): O(V + E). Shortest path in
  unweighted graphs.
- **DFS** (depth-first search): O(V + E). Connectivity, cycle
  detection, topological sort.
- **Dijkstra:** shortest path with non-negative weights. O((V+E) log V).
- **Bellman-Ford:** handles negative weights. O(VE).
- **Kruskal / Prim:** minimum spanning tree.

**Common graph types:**

- $K_n$: complete graph.
- $C_n$: cycle on $n$ vertices.
- Tree, forest.
- Bipartite (vertices in two groups, edges only across).
- DAG (directed acyclic graph): used for dependencies.

:::

> *Graphs = vertices + edges; degree = edge count at a vertex;*
> *Trees = minimally connected; BFS / DFS / Dijkstra: classic
> traversals;*
> *Graphs model anything with structured pairwise relationships.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A graph has 7 vertices and degrees $4, 4, 3, 3, 2, 2, 2$. How many
> edges?

Sum of degrees: $4 + 4 + 3 + 3 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 20$. So $|E| = 10$
(handshake lemma).

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Edges in $K_5$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\binom{5}{2} = 10$.
</details>

**W2.** A tree on 100 vertices has how many edges?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$99$.
</details>

**W3.** Sum of degrees if a graph has 8 edges?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$16$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Is the graph with degrees $1, 2, 2, 3, 3$ possible?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sum = 11 — odd. By handshake lemma, must be even.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**No** — odd sum impossible.
</details>

**P2.** How does DFS detect a cycle in an undirected graph?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
While DFS-traversing, if you reach a vertex you've visited *that
isn't your parent in the DFS tree*, you've found a cycle.
</details>

**P3.** Web pages and hyperlinks: graph or not? Directed or
undirected?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Directed graph.** Hyperlinks are one-way (page A → page B doesn't
imply B → A). PageRank (T5 L04 S2) uses this directed graph.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **The seven bridges of Königsberg** (Euler, 1735): 7 bridges
connect 4 landmasses. Can you walk a path crossing each bridge
exactly once?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**No.** An *Eulerian path* exists iff the graph has exactly 0 or 2
vertices of odd degree (and is connected). Königsberg's graph has 4
odd-degree vertices.

This 1735 problem launched **graph theory** as a discipline. Euler's
proof was the first major result.
</details>

**S2.** **Why are graphs central to modern AI?** Graph Neural
Networks (GNNs) operate on graph-structured data: molecules, social
networks, knowledge graphs, recommender systems. Why is this
abstraction useful?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Graphs naturally model relations.** A molecule = graph of atoms +
bonds; a social network = graph of users + friendships; a knowledge
graph = entities + relations.

GNNs apply neural-network message-passing along edges, learning
representations that respect graph structure. This is now state-of-
the-art for many ML problems where the data has natural graph
structure.

Graph theory you just learned (BFS-style traversals, edge
representations) underlies the *implementation* of every GNN.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Graph = the picture."** A graph is mathematically just $V$ and
   $E$; the *drawing* is a visualisation. The same graph has many
   drawings.
2. **"BFS vs DFS — what's the difference?"** BFS explores neighbours
   first (queue, shortest path). DFS goes deep first (stack /
   recursion, used for connectivity, cycles, topological sort).
3. **"Trees are about plants."** In CS / maths, *trees* are connected
   acyclic graphs. The biological metaphor stuck.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state the handshake lemma?
2. Without scrolling back, how many edges in $K_6$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $\binom{6}{2} = 15$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[13 · Modular Arithmetic & RSA](../13-modular-arithmetic-rsa/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S2** (GNNs).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[11 · Sets, Logic, Proofs](../11-sets-logic-proofs/index.qmd)**
- **[T3 L11 — Combinatorics](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/11-combinatorics-counting/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- **Tier 6 — Graph Theory** (advanced) — colourings, planarity,
  matching theorems, spectral graph theory.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** Graph databases (Neo4j, Neptune) store entities and
relationships natively. Many query problems reduce to graph traversal.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** GNNs (S2). Knowledge graphs.
Computational graphs in deep learning frameworks.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Systems & networks.** Routing protocols (OSPF uses Dijkstra),
DNS, social network analysis, dependency resolution (package managers,
build systems).
:::
